text: Here we present a stepwise approach to laparoscopic right hemicolectomy with DII
Our patient is a six year old male with the following medical history.
The tumour can be visualised on this CT scan.
The patient is positioned supine on the operating table, and laparoscopic ports are placed according to the surgical diagram.
After port placement, the patient is positioned in a Trendelenburg position with the head tilted down and the right shoulder elevated.
The surgeon moves the omentum into the right upper quadrant above the liver, while positioning the small bowel into the left side of the abdomen.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection of the ascending colon, carefully separating the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon prepares to separate the duodenum and achieve vessel control, focusing on these key stages of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon initiates dissection beneath the ileocolic pedicle, focusing on separating the anatomical structures in this region.
After making an initial incision into the peritoneum, the surgeon enters the correct anatomical plane, revealing the underlying structures for further dissection.
The surgeon dissects a bloodless plane using a combination of hooked diathermy and blunt dissection, effectively separating tissue layers without bleeding.
The surgeon progresses towards the duodenum, separating retroperitoneal structures and fascia from the mesocolon using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon exposes important retroperitoneal structures, including the inferior vena cava (IVC), gonadal vessels, and ureter, during the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and separates it from the mesocolon using scissors and blunt dissection, carefully manipulating the tissue to maintain a bloodless plane.
The surgeon carefully avoids using diathermy near the duodenum to prevent thermal injury, maintaining a safe distance from the delicate tissue.
After fully mobilizing the duodenum, the surgeon proceeds with a medial to lateral dissection of the mesocolon, separating it from adjacent retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel along with a gentle sweeping motion to separate the retroperitoneal fascia from the mesocolic envelope, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The video shows a clear color delineation marking the borders between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum, highlighting the distinct separation between these anatomical structures.
A swab is placed above the duodenum following its separation from the mesocolon, marking the completion of this stage of the dissection.
This acts as a landmark during later stages of the mobilisation.
The surgeon displays the mobilized duodenum, the head of the pancreas, and retroperitoneal structures, all separated from the mesocolon.
The surgeon begins vessel control by addressing the ileocolic pedicle, preparing to manage the blood supply to the colon.
This diagram shows common anatomical variations.
The surgeon skeletonizes the ileocolic pedicle, carefully exposing the superior mesenteric vein to prepare for safe division.
The surgeon applies counter traction by pulling the ileocolic pedicle towards the patient's right side, aiding in the exposure and manipulation of the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon creates tension on the peritoneal coverings, facilitating their division, with visible separation of the tissue layers.
The surgeon places haemlock clips on the fully exposed ileocolic pedicle, ensuring vessel control after skeletonizing and exposing the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon places haemlock clips as proximal as possible on the exposed ileocolic pedicle, ensuring adherence to oncological resection principles.
The surgeon applies two proximal clips and one distal clip to the exposed vessel, then divides the vessel between the clips.
The surgeon places additional clips on structures within the surgical field, which may or may not contain vessels, to minimize the risk of bleeding during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon controls the ileocolic artery and vein using clips, as they lie close to each other, ensuring both vessels are securely managed.
After dividing the ileocolic pedicle, the surgeon proceeds to divide the tributaries of the superior mesenteric vein, using surgical instruments to carefully separate these vessels.
There is often anatomical variation with these branches.
However, the principle of central venous ligation remains the same.
The surgeon identifies the branches of the superior mesenteric vein and applies clips as proximally as possible to control these vessels and minimize the risk of bleeding.
This ensures a maximum lymph node yield and hence a more favourable oncological outcome.
The surgeon controls the vessels to gain mobility, facilitating the dissection above the duodenum.
The surgeon completes the dissection by creating a visible window into the lesser sac, marking the end point of this stage.
This can be seen here.
The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue planes involved in the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon exposes the duodenum and the head of the pancreas, revealing these anatomical structures clearly within the surgical field.
The surgeon safely ligates any remaining vessels, ensuring complete control and mobility for the dissection above the duodenum.
The surgeon identifies a vessel likely to be a tributary of the gastrocolonic trunk, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a subalial dissection to fully mobilize the cecum and terminal ileum from the posterior fascia, ensuring these structures are free from their attachments.
The surgeon elevates the cecum and appendix to create tension, facilitating entry into an avascular plane for dissection.
The surgeon applies traction and counter traction to create tension, facilitating the division of fibers between the posterior fascia and the mesocolic envelope of the cecum.
The surgeon continues the dissection to create a window that connects the subalial dissection with the previously performed medial to lateral dissection.
Care needs to be taken to avoid injuring the posterior structures, principally the gonadal vessels and the ureter.
The surgeon creates a window by dissecting between the subalveolar and medial to lateral planes, revealing the avascular space between the posterior fascia and the mesocolic envelope of the cecum.
The surgeon successfully joins the medial to lateral dissection with the subalial dissection, visually connecting these two dissection planes.
The surgeon highlights the close proximity of the gonadal vessels and the ureter located posteriorly during the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection until reaching the swab placed over the duodenum, marking the termination of this stage of the procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon, utilizing traction and counter-traction techniques to carefully manipulate and separate the tissue.
The assistant pulls the colon to the patient's left side, creating tension that facilitates the dissection process.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the colon, ensuring the mesocolic envelope remains intact during the dissection.
The surgeon continues lateral mobilisation of the colon, revealing the right lobe of the liver as the dissection progresses.
This marks the end of this stage of the procedure.
Full hepatic flexure mobilisation has been described in previous videos.
The surgeon separates the omentum from the colon, maintaining tension on the colon to facilitate dissection.
The surgeon creates a window into the lesser sac between the colon and omentum, visually separating these structures to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon continues the dissection in a medial to lateral direction, progressing towards the previously established line of dissection from the lateral mobilisation, with the right lobe of the liver visible.
The surgeon divides the remaining vessels with the colon fully mobilized, ensuring safe separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies a vessel, likely the right gastroepiploic vein, within the surgical field.
The colon is visibly positioned at the patient's midline, indicating successful mobilization and repositioning during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon exposes the underlying posterior structures, with haemlocks visible on the centrally ligated vessels, indicating successful vessel ligation.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a small midline incision, utilizing an Alexis wound protector to maintain the incision site.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis of the colon using a TLS-CMT and TA-CMT linear stapler, following the standard Barcelona technique.
